steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The researchers concluded that net CO₂ is likely to increase when seasonal snow arrives earlier relative to the onset of soil surface freezing. A supporting finding must explain the mechanism: why does early snow drive net CO₂ up? The passage tells us CO₂ rises when heterotrophic respiration (output) outpaces plant absorption (uptake). The correct choice must show that early snow favors respiration over plant growth.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘Relatively early snow cover reduces the amount of soil moisture available for the growth of plant species such as *O. secunda* and lowers the rate of heterotrophic respiration.’: lower respiration would reduce CO₂ output, not increase it — this contradicts the conclusion. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘The soil insulation provided by snow cover enables heterotrophic respiration to continue during a period in which plant species such as *O. secunda* are typically not growing.’: if snow arrives before soil freezes, it insulates the soil and keeps it warm enough for soil microbes to keep releasing CO₂, while plants remain dormant and cannot absorb it. Output continues; uptake does not. Net CO₂ rises. This directly supports the conclusion. ✓
  • Choice C — ‘The effect of soil moisture on the growth of vegetation and on the rate of heterotrophic respiration is stronger during the period of seasonal snow cover than during the period without snow cover.’: addresses the relative strength of soil-moisture effects, not the timing of snow arrival and its impact on the respiration–growth balance. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘Seasonal snow cover tends to persist longer in areas of relatively low vegetation growth and high heterotrophic respiration than in areas of relatively high vegetation growth and low heterotrophic respiration.’: about where snow lingers, not when it arrives — irrelevant to the conclusion about early snow arrival. ✗
That confirms B is correct.
